comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a quote again: 
 
"We must realize two things, then, if we are to avail ourselves of the ancient wisdom.  Firstly, mental and emotional conditions have power to affect us without overt physical action; secondly, we must not allow reactions to these influences to gallop about like wild horses, but must break them in to harness.  To perceive an influence is not necessarily to react to it.  There is such a thing as counteracting it, and it is this which occult wisdom teaches us to do." 
 
That's from Dion Fortune's Practical Occultism in Daily Life, one of the books of hers that did an enormous amount to shape my understanding of magic and the occult.   

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will probably be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed.  See you next week!***

[IMAGE:1]

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com!

replies.title: Re: Polytheistic Ceremonial Magic + Wheel of Life
replies.content:

Golden Dawn rituals work best with the Tree of Life. If you wanted to go further, my books The Celtic Golden Dawn and The Mysteries of Merlin would give you a very good idea of what you could do, and of course the rest of the GD literature would be the next step.

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Polytheistic Ceremonial Magic + Wheel of Life

replies.title: Re: Polytheistic Ceremonial Magic + Wheel of Life

replies.title: Re: Polytheistic Ceremonial Magic + Wheel of Life
replies.content: You don't have to go further. The material I put in the essay is enough to provide a good basic training system for an entire lifetime. comments.title: Daoist immortal and The man from earth
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I missed the discussion on the Daoist immortal last week, but it did spur me to think about it.
That a person could trough a combination of physical conditioning, diet, inner alchemy and mind power attain longevity for millenia. Even stranger, that such a person would relinkquish such a life and return to a normal cycle.
Why? Would such an existance not be an immense oppurtunety to learn and develop?
One of my favourite movies is the 2007 movie “The man from earth”. Discussing what a man who could live 14000 years would look like.
Quote: “what a chance to learn, when I learned to learn”

And why would such a person return as a normal person to the cycle afterwards, would a millenia worth of wisdom not advance your development?

Or would it only allow you to perfect one personality, but contribute just one step towards the development of the individuality. And so the recurrence of the life cycles teaches more then one long life.
That the point is not to be one great personality, but to be an individuality that will, due to its strong character, always build itself a balanced incarnation?

Or maybe the process involves so much work and personal sacrifice, isolation. That it ultimatly becomes a “longer life, but not more life” situation. Yes you live on, but your hermit hut is a prison of ritual.

Thank you for your reply,
V

P.S.: sorry for the rambling comment. And please, this is taken as a continuation to a post from last week. If my rambling questions should be offensive or to close for comfort for the OP, please delete my comment.

replies.title: Re: Daoist immortal and The man from earth
replies.content: I think your latter two speculations are close to the mark. My experience in my own life is that any personality becomes a burden eventually; I've still got plenty I want to do in this life, but I can sense very clearly that when the time comes to let go of this body and this personality, I'll do it with a sigh of relief. And of course longer life does not necessarily mean more life; Bilbo Baggins' comments about feeling spread too thin by the Ring seem relevant.

replies.title: Re: Daoist immortal and The man from earth
replies.content: Still I can't help but think, wouldn't it be nice to know life extending rituals? If they do eat up the entirety of your time though, its no fun. On the other hand, I guess when you get to a certain level of development and you can remember you past lives it no longer matters. On the third hand why hold on to a mortal carcass when you can live in an immortal body of light. The only question is how to get from where I am today to that level. "Through daily hard work and dedication" I expect JMG to say. :-P

replies.title: Re: Daoist immortal and The man from earth
replies.content: Hard work, dedication, careful study, thoughtful reflection, and clearing away negative karma while doing your level best not to pile up any more.  I know, it's quite a laundry list.

replies.title: Re: Daoist immortal and The man from earth
replies.content:
This came by right as I was commiting my meditations to the concept of individuality as separate from the personality.
So it is an exercise to think of an extreme case of an extended personality.
I must admit I am still strugling to understand it. What it is? Character?

The idea of contacting your higher self. Is the individuality like a second half of us, one that is concies and active, or is it the sum of our latent potential on the upper mental and spiritual planes, that we have to develop?

replies.title: Re: Daoist immortal and The man from earth
replies.content: The Individuality is who you really are. The personality is the mask you're wearing in this incarnation. The more you can see your personality as simply a set of habits of thought and action -- which is in fact all it is -- and your real self as something different from it, the closer you get to realization.

replies.title: Re: Daoist immortal and The man from earth
replies.content:
So you would imagine yourself as somebody observing your personality, just like you would observe your body. Like you would observe your body being obese, you would see certain patterns in your personality, like irritability, and would take steps to mitigate them. Like practicing stoicism to keep yourself from overreacting, and mentally looking for constructive solutions, to redirect your efforts.
In time this exercise becomes your new modus operandi. And your personality shifts. Like your body weight would shift with regular exercise.

You get an immediate result by making yourself more constructive and less stress prone in this personality. In the mean time your individuality learns the same lesson, plus you lay down a pattern of being able to observe your personality. Leaving it as an option for a later personality to use from the onset.

The later personality then needs to come to the same realization, easier for it since it has the tracks laid down already, that it is a mask and so can easily learn do the same as the previous one. A mention of Dion Fortune, that she regained memories of her practicing magic fairly fast, when she picked up the practice comes to mind. + she had some residual character traits that made her very firm in her standards.

I am guessing this all becomes clearer once you regain some memories and can see the different incarnations and a pattern laid down trough them. The single instances are like steps in the evolutionary path.

Uff, work. Stumbling in the dark.

replies.title: Re: Daoist immortal and The man from earth
replies.content: Not stumbling in the dark at all.  This is basically correct.

replies.title: Re: Daoist immortal and The man from earth
replies.content:
That's been my experience in my own life, too: "any personality becomes a burden eventually."

I used the current lockdown productively to write down, primarily for my children and grandchild, all the old family stories that I inherited while growing up. (It turned out to be the longest piece of writing I've ever done in all my years as an academic.) That feels like it was the last major task I was meant to accomplish during my (current) lifetime--though time will tell whether there's another such task ahead of me that I don't see clearly just yet.

My mother died last November at 106 1/2 years of age. (Both my wife and I come from very long-lived stock.) During my mother's last few years, what remained of her was definitely "spread too thin," and she was impatient for her life to be over. I don't doubt for a moment that she stopped living "with a sigh of relief" that her life was finally over.

comments.title: Re enchanting North America
comments.content:
JMG

Good evening.
In North America we have no access to, or complete knowledge, of sacred sites in our lands. Often it has been mentioned on your sites that we ruined our chances at this because of the treatment of the indigenous people.
If you take John Michelle's view as fact - that because we haven't been permitted to build our sacred temples and churches on the sacred locations in North America that we therefore have a disturbed and unstable populace (among other problems) - then:

1. would you agree that the recent maelstorms of citizen discontent and chaos are ,in part, due to the issue John Michelle pointed out?

2. Would you consider magical workings that aim towards reconciliation with indigenous safe?

3. Would you agree that the greatest work concerning re enchanting the world, in North America, would ultimately involve building new structures on these ancient sites?

My apologies for the long questions this has been in the back of my head for a few weeks. I can't decide if Michelle was expanding this problem beyond reasonable limits, but I feel he is very much on to something that North Americans need to address for the health future ect.

replies.title: Re: Re enchanting North America
replies.content:
1) I think that's an important part of the background. There's much more going on, of course.

2) Only with the permission and cooperation of the indigenous peoples -- and you're going to have a hard time getting that. To a very real extent, we made the mess, we get to live with it.

3) Emphatically not. Something else John Michell points out is relevant here: the paths and shrines of the earth spirit are not fixed in place forever; in each age of the world they shift and take on new alignments. That's the magical reason why civilizations fall -- the sacred places that give them their link to divinity eventually lose their sacred quality, and new sacred places had to be found. What we have to do -- a hugely difficult task, but one that our history has made inescapable -- is to watch for the emergence of new constellations of sacred places, and then be prepared to figure out how to work with them. Several of my books are intended as first steps in that direction.

replies.title: Re: Re enchanting North America

comments.content:
I'm normally reading MM later in a week so I wanted to comment on a few threads from last Monday before asking a couple of questions to JMG.

1. A question to Robert Mathiesen if that's OK. You posted an impressive description of how you would work with a restless ancestor if you had one. How would your approach change if instead of an ancestor it was a parasitic entity that has been with a family line for generations? The kind that lives on a single host and works mostly through them but also can feed through people who spend enough time with the host to adopt some very particular negative behaviour patterns towards themselves and others. Would you use a similar approach to send this kind of entity away from a non-host family member and their surroundings?

2. Someone mentioned last week about one of their possible past life as a Chinese alchemist who lived 1000 years. The commenter expressed doubt as to whether something like that is possible at all. I just wanted to mention here a documented account of Li Ching-Yuen - a Chinese Taoist who lived for more than 200 years and who left his life voluntarily when he decided that he's done enough with this incarnation. Several years ago even Wikipedia article about him stated that according to verified documents he lived for 256 years. These days the article is heavily edited and his account is called a myth and a hoax but it might still be a good starting point for a research.

3. There was another conversation last and previous MM around what gods really receive from us when we make our offerings. I was asking this question in my prayers some time ago and what came back was an image of my cat bumping his head softly into mine. He often comes to me and shows his affection in his cat ways and I was basically told that what I get from my cat in those moments (even when he stomps on my keyboard and spills my tea) is roughly what gods are getting from me.

4. And now a question to JMG. Some time ago on MM someone was telling a story of a creepy colleague of theirs who was quite clearly into one of the nastier corners of magic and who was trying to fish for the patron god of the commenter. The commenter said that they softly evaded the question and didn't provide the information thinking that it could be used to screw them over. My question is, how can the knowledge of what god someone prays to be used against them? To clarify, I am not looking for an instruction and I would be only thankful if you omit any details that might help someone to curse anyone. I am simply trying to understand how that is even possible, how an attack like that can be recognised by a target, and how to fend against it. I have a mild suspicion that I could have been a target of a similar attack several years ago but I'm not sure how to verify it and I wasn't even aware that it's a possibility until it got mentioned here.

5. And another question to JMG. Or not so much a question but a request to validate or trash a line of thinking. Last week, one commenter mentioned a podcast where a guest was saying how LBRP was inadequate to keep out the demon they've summoned straight after performing a banishing ritual. I think I've listened to that podcast a while ago too and I had half a question lingering after the conversation that happened here last week. Setting aside the lunacy of summoning a malign entity straight after banishing, would it be fair to say that the problem with LBRP was not so much with it being not strong enough but rather in it being a wrong class of a tool for the job? My impression from his words on that podcast was that the guest took LBRP as a way of tracing an impassable circle. Theoretically speaking one could come up with UBRP (Uber Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram) that is 1000 times stronger than the lesser form and the way I understand it is that it would still not be the same thing as creating a barrier that cannot be passed by a demon. It's one thing to sweep a floor in a kitchen daily to avoid food scraps attracting vermin and it's something else when one wants to bring a hungry hyena into their house. Whatever their reasoning is for doing something like that, a caged area with metal bars would be a more appropriate prop for the job than clean floors no matter how clean and deodorised they are. Does this reasoning make sense?

ganeshling:

I don't know (yet) how I would deal with a situation like that; I haven't met up with such a parasite so far.

To explain a little bit further about my ignorance ... I've written in the past about my family's view that (almost) every adult human contains a multitude of "waking selves" that can be at odds with one another: the trick of becoming a functioning human is to learn as much as you can about these selves of yours, so you can wake up the self best suited to each new situation you find yourself in, and put the previous self down "for a nap," so to speak. (There's more to it than that, of course; there are also selves you never want to let out of their "dungeon cells.")

But a human also has more than just this panoply of many alternate waking selves inside, or at least I do. One such is a being I call the "Teacher Within." He steps in when I am called to deal with a situation that needs magic, but I have no idea how to proceed. Then, all of a sudden, I know exactly what to do, and it seems to me that I have always had that knowledge, but had simply mislaid it up to now. Yet I am sure that I have never consciously learned that knowledge, or even read about it anywhere. (This gives a very strange feeling, absolutely unmistakable and like nothing else.) When this happens, I do what comes to me, and it seems to work.

The point of this rambling of mine is to explain why I don't have an answer to your question: I've never met up with such a parasite, and don't remember reading about any means to get rid of one that struck me as effective. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Certain magic squares are important emblems of the seven planetary energies -- the 3x3 square is assigned to Saturn, a 4x4 to Jupiter, and so on in order to a 9x9 for the Moon. They were apparently invented by the Babylonians, and most modern traditions of ceremonial magic still use them. You can draw them on talismans, and you can also use them as matrices for the construction of sigils -- patterns of lines that represent certain words and names.

replies.title: How to find a friend
replies.content:
A young man, about my son's age, was in a carpool with me for a few weeks. After hearing that I was in my second marriage, he said that he was lonely, and asked if I could give him any advice about how to find a nice woman for a serious relationship. My advice was "don't try to do it alone. If you have one friend, or acquaintance, or mentor, let them know what you want. They may know of some suitable woman who wants the same thing. But if you're all keeping your desires to yourself, you'll never make a connection." That was, IIRC, our conversation on Thursday.

On Monday morning, he was ecstatic. He had described our conversation to a group of classmates, and a woman among them said "I had no idea! I'd be happy to make a date with you. I just thought you were too focused on your studies." And, so it began.

So, accept your awkwardness, and relax. Bring something, such as a tip about a book you enjoyed reading, or music you heard. What do you know that others might like to know? What do you cook that others might like to eat? Where have you been that others might like to go? The common theme here is "what excites you about your own (solitary) life?" If you can't answer that question now, that's something to work on.

Lathechuck

comments.title: Miscellaneous questions
comments.content:
Hi John, I have a few fairly random compiled questions which I'vehad over the past few weeks, if you don't mind answering them?


- Western elementals are earth, wind, fire and water, but how does the Eastern system with metal and wood (and sometimes void), but no air, work with elementals?

- Are auras and the ethic body just different names for the same thing?

replies.title: Re: Miscellaneous questions
replies.content:
1) The elements are categories. In some languages there's no word for the color orange -- what we call orange, they call dark yellow. The different systems of elements are the same way, just different ways of dividing up a spectrum of forces and beings.

2) I assume you mean the etheric body! The aura is partly etheric and partly astral, so you're half right.

3) I have no idea what you're asking here. The word "Spirit" does not mean the same thing as the phrase "praying to the universe." Perhaps you can explain.

4) I explained that here.

5) Plants that are used to growing in shade make good houseplants, and yes, they have a positive effect on the etheric level.

6) Goats are associated with black magic because the Christians took the god Pan and claimed that he was their Devil. (This was part of the embarrassingly common Christian habit of bearing false witness against their non-Christian neighbors.) Since Pan looks like a goat-human hybrid, the goat symbolism got picked up more generally and used as a symbol for anything bad.

replies.title: Re: AODA & Masonry
replies.content:
Nearly all the Masonic lodges that have female members belong to what's called Co-Masonry, which has been closely connected with the Theosophical Society for well over a century. Ask your friend if it's a Co-Masonic lodge; if it is, you can tell them everything -- most of the members will be up to their eyeballs in occultism.

On the other hand, if it's not a Co-Masonic lodge, you probably don't want to mention magic around them at all -- and if it's one of the women's lodges connected to Freemasonry, such as Eastern Star, do not breathe a word about magic. Most of the members will be Christians of the ordinary "magic is devil worship" variety.

comments.title: Crime and punishment
comments.content:
What would be a magically appropriate way of dealing with severe crimes? (I don't mean through hexes, etc, but punishment on the physical plane) Should the offender of say, a murder, be executed swiftly and dispassionately, so that they can face judgement and penance in the afterlife and their next lives? (As well as 'getting them out of the way' and preventing further harm) Or be made to suffer, whether through hard labour or actual torture so they can 'work off' some of their karma? Or be simply locked away, alone, to face their own conscience? Or, finally, the so-called 'restorative justice' route of community service, meeting the victim's family, reflecting on their crimes, etc.

On a related note, what does performing an execution do to one's karma? Yes, you are killing someone, but a) it is to prevent further harm (assuming justice has actually been done and the wrong person hasn't been convicted) and b) it's not by your own choice, as the court has decided to execute them.

replies.title: Re: Crime and punishment
replies.content:
It really depends. I'm in favor of capital punishment for the worst crimes, and that should be quick, dispassionate, and clean, as much because of what anything else would do to the egregor of society as for any other reason. The goal here is to take someone who's unfit to live among human beings and send him to his destiny. For less serious felonies, prison with labor (not hard labor, but not sitting around in a cell watching television!) is a good way to let them work off some karma, pay back some of their debt to society, and pick up some job skills so that crime isn't the only option once they get out. Restorative justice should only be tried for first-time offenders who commit nonviolent crimes, and if they reoffend they should get the prison sentence for the original crime as well as the new one -- thus they have a real incentive not to screw up! Meanwhile, a great many "crimes" (such as smoking pot) should not be crimes at all.

As for the executioner's karma, it's pretty much the same as the soldier's karma -- a very complicated mix of negative and positive.

comments.title: Affirmations
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I was reading your old blog posts about affirmations, and one thing I don't quite understand is why affirmations work to begin with. Do you agree with the modern school of CBT practitioners that our thoughts shape our feelings and perceptions of the world, or as Epictetus said "People are not disturbed by things, but by the views they take of them"? But are thoughts the only things that shape our perception and worldview? Is it not possible that some of that material originates in the unconscious and is, so to speak, beyond the reach of the conscious mind?

Secondly, how malleable is the mind through affirmations? Can we make ourselves believe anything we want?

replies.title: Re: Affirmations
replies.content:
1) Thoughts aren't the only things that shape our perception and worldview, but affirmations are designed to go below the threshold of thought and set up patterns in what old-fashioned occultists used to call the subjective mind and psychologists later termed the subconscious. That's why you repeat them over and over again, and why you don't have to believe them in order for them to be effective.

2) There are very few limits, if any, to what you can make yourself believe. The difficulty, of course, is that the world does not care what you believe about it. You can convince yourself that cars do not exist; that won't prevent you from being run down and killed if you take a stroll on the empty freeway. Thus affirmations need to be used with quite some care!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
In late June, I had an infectious illness, not you-know-what but with similar symptoms (as I later found out). On the nights following the two worst days, body and joint aches made it difficult to find any comfortable position to try to sleep in.

My dreams can be as odd and varied as anyone’s, but in my dreams I’m always either myself, or a pretty much identity-less observer. When I have a fever my dreams tend to become anxious and repetitive, but no different in regards to “who I am” in the dream. I’ve never dreamed I was some celebrity or other specific individual, or a dog or a bird or a tree.

Except for those two nights in late June. Then, I dreamed repeatedly that I was the stretch of intertidal cattail bed along the river nearby. I wan’t in it or looking at it; I was it, a feature of the landscape. While I was the cattail bed, bounded and shaped by the river’s flow (being the right shape was important, in the dream), I could rest comfortably.

Waking up was disconcerting, though. It felt less like waking up than it did like remembering I had my own identity and a human body and returning to them, though I had no sensation of motion. Instead, my body and the elongated shape of the cattail bed seemed briefly superimposed in the transition.

The morning after the second night I felt much better, and continued recovering after that.

1. How should the involvement of physical illness (in this case, a systemic bacterial infection) affect the interpretation or significance of a mysterious experience? I’m not inclined to discount it as meaningless for that reason, but as some people have suggested about drug experiences, physiologically altered perceptions might not be trustworthy. (Speaking of drugs, I had not taken any sleep aid or pain relieving medications those nights.)

2. Largely from your writings, I know about half a dozen ways to act to prevent such experiences (not willed by me) from happening. But, should I? Leaving my body hardly seems safe. More like the definition of the opposite. But it seems benevolent powers were involved in this case. A door cracked open, or a perilous abyss? (Peril would be, e.g., my wife ending up bereft and lonely, even if I’m happy as a clam wherever I am.)

As usual, I’ve mostly answered my own questions writing this, but I appreciate your perspective so I’ll post this anyhow. I began with the intent to seek a relationship with local nature spirits, while refraining from prior expectations. Now shale is steadily getting more real, and this Narnia stuff, exchanging gifts with birds and astrally napping with the cattails, certainly qualifies as nothing I expected. So, making progress, it seems.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Illness can make you much more open to the astral plane; I'm not sure anyone knows why, but there it is. It doesn't seem to predispose people to false dreams, though.

2) Leaving your body is perfectly safe. You do it every night when you dream. You're getting the response you've asked for, and will likely be getting much more of it; I'd say that counts as a success.

comments.title: How to interact with spirit of a place

I live near a state forest in lovely New England that has a small pond on the ridge of the mountain that I've always loved. My family goes there often to hang out, and for our youngster to splash in the shallows. Recently we were there and I observed that for whatever reason, the pond is a little pocket of calm. replies.content:
Rook was invented by a fundamentalist Christian, I forget which denomination, because regular playing cards are evil because they can be used for gambling. (So could Rook, although I don’t know if anyone does.). Rook is still popular in the south; for one thing it’s super-simple. You can learn how to play in about 5 minutes. The deck has 4 suits—red, black, green, and yellow —plus a rook, the high trump. 2 sets of partners try to take tricks. Each set of partners bids for the right to name the trump color. Once trump color for that hand is determined, play proceeds as with any other trick-taking game. If the trump declarers don’t take at least the number of points in their bid, they are set back that number of points.

Rook was a great game to have around on holidays when many generations were mingled; since it’s so simple, even kids could play. Cards were numbered 1-14 in each suit, so a bored kid could even take out the 4 14’s and play Pyramid with the rest of the deck.

A while back I was Internet-shopping and saw Rook advertised. Curious, I clicked over. The decks were priced around $20, but in reading the reviews I saw a number of people complaining that the card stock was so thin you could read the card face through the back! Sigh. Modern America [unDruidly word]s up everything, even a simple and innocent pastime like Rook.

—Lady Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Cylces of Time, Progress, and Christianity
replies.content: Oswald Spengler pointed out that the rationalism of every culture is its religion with the serial numbers filed off. That's true in this case, certainly -- the cult of progress is simply warmed-over Christianity. You can see that most clearly in the Transhumanist end of progress-worshipers, where literally every detail of Protestant fundamentalist theology is present and accounted for under a thin layer of technological drag, but the entire theme of salvation through progress is a warmed-over rehash of Christian theology.

replies.title: Re: Cylces of Time, Progress, and Christianity

In the postmillennialist analysis, Christ established his kingdom on Earth during the Incarnation, and as Christians evangelize the world, things will indeed get better and better until the Millennium arrives; then, after a thousand Utopian years, Christ will return. Wikipedia, surprisingly, has a decent article on the subject.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm currently in the process of reading The Secret Doctrine and it occurred to me that the cosmic process of creation or emanation it describes, which proceeds from the ideal to the concrete, is parallelled by human process of creation—for instance, artistic creation. When I write something, I start out with vague ideas about what the thing I write will be about, or what will happen in the story, and it takes some work to figure out which concrete set of letters and punctuation marks will best incarnate those ideas and make other people able to perceive them, so to speak.

This also calls to mind a comment I've heard from artists and non-artists alike, that limitations in the creation of some piece of art (be they economic, technical, or creative limitations) can help the final product turn out good. It occurs to me that this is because an act of creation inherently involves taking vague ideas about what one wants to create and bringing them down to the level of concrete, differentiated, physical reality, with all sorts of laws telling you that "you can't do this like that" or "you can't do that at all". Without limitations, it's all too easy to allow those vague, formless ideas to manifest as a vague, formless mess instead of putting in the hard work to give them an appealing shape, and so the final product comes out as a potential masterpiece but a concrete mess.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Excellent! Certainly in my experience emanationist cosmology does a very good job of tracking the creative process.

As for limitation, exactly. It's a lot easier to write a good sonnet than to write good free verse, for example, because the sonnet form forces you out of your unthinking habits of thought and speech -- you have to find words that rhyme and scan, so it's more likely that you'll hit on something genuinely interesting.

comments.title: Earth currents/sacred power
comments.content:
Earlier in this post, you talked about places losing their power due to changing currents. Is this an entirely natural process, or can humans make it worse? Stonehenge losing its power because it is for most people just an archaeological curiousity, not a sacred space?
There is a natural granite outcropping a bit west of Austin called Enchanted Rock. So named, the tourist guides tell us, “because the Native Americans believed it had magical powers”. Of course, you can hear the condescending voice in which it is written, right? The thing is, on my first visit there, I got there sort of by accident (stuck in a small hill country town with nothing to do, looking at a map and saying what’s this enchanted rock thing? Lets go look at it) and even though I knew nothing about it other than its name on a (paper!) map (to answer the obvious question in younger people’s mind: the internet, much less google, had not been invented yet) I felt like the place had power. This was several decades ago. My most recent visit, two years ago, was depressing. The place felt dead, and what little feeble energy was left was tired and hostile. Is this a result of natural changes, or too many years of thousands of clueless tourists tromping all over it?
Of immediate concern to me: In the past year I started attending services at an old church in our town. It was built before WWI and has been in use continuously. The people who built it obviously understood consecrated ground: it has a “feel” about it like nothing else in the area. It is aligned north/south and its interior dimensions, i just realized the other day, match solomon’s temple. It is built on the highest elevation in town (not saying much: this place is pretty flat). What worries me is this: most of the people there don’t take it seriously. We have choir members leaving their sippy cups on the choir railing, people sitting there sharing videos on their phones before (and sometimes during!) service. Even the rector seems to not take god seriously, seeming to think god is an abstract concept, not a real being. So my worry/question is this: how to protect/preserve the place? I feel like there is something I should be doing. I’ve prayed about it, because I’m quite sure I was led here for a reason. But the answer I seem to be getting so far is “don’t ask why. Read. Learn.” Hm. I guess I just answered my own question. If I’m meant to do anything I’ll probably be guided by the one who led me there, right?

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
(Tolkienguy)

A somewhat strange question occurred to me...can a basically nonreligous society like ours deify someone without realizing what its doing?

I'm thinking here of the story of Chris McCandless. For those who haven't heard of him, Chris McCandless was a man from a well-off (but apparently abusive) middle class family in Northern Virginia. Shortly after graduating college, when he would have been expected to find a nice middle class job and settle down, McCandless cut off all contact with his family and friends and began wandering around the United States-first in his car, but he eventually abandoned it in a gully and travelled by hitchiking or on foot. After several years, he eventually made his way to the vicinity of Fairbanks, Alaska, where he decided to hike over the mountains to the Pacific Ocean. Unfortunately, he lacked both knowledge of the outdoors and an adequate food supply, and wound up starving to death in an abandoned bus used as a shelter by hunters.

Some time later, in the early 2000's, noted outdoor writer John Krakauer became taken with McCandless's story and authored a book about his life, which portrayed McCandless as a sort of spiritual seeker-someone who had rejected modern society and strove to forge a path and meaning of his own, and ultimately died in the attempt. Krakauer's book became popular-it was used in college reading discussions (where I first encountered it), Indie rock bands wrote songs about McCandless, and the book eventually got made into an arthouse film. Furthermore, people began hiking out into the Alaskan wilderness to visit the bus McCandless died in. Unfortunately, the bus is in a rather hard to reach area-one has to cross a fast flowing river to get to it-and as the McCandless "pilgrimage" became more and more popular, multiple people died in the attempt. Even after this was publicized, people kept trying to visit the bus, in a way that eerily reminds one of Catholic pilgrims struggling to reach a martyr's tomb. (Here's a rather perplexed BBC article on the phenomenon: https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-50003559 )

When I first heard the McCandless story, I did not really understand why people were so taken with it-he came off to me as a troubled kid who did something really unwise and died as a result. The more I think about it, though, the more I'm convinced that something of a magical-religious nature is going on here, though I can't figure out quite what. Has some sort of archetype manifested through McCandless and the following that's grown around him? The way people treated that bus almost reminds me of the way people in established religions treat their sacred spaces-do you think there's any connection? Are people just so desperate to escape the American middle class life that they've made an idol out of a foolish kid who got himself lost in the wilderness? What do you think?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's an interesting question to which I don't have an answer. It's a fascinating phenomenon, though.

I think that's *exactly* what that McCandless story is tapping, especially with that "breaching the everyday society he was raised in to forge his own path" framing - for a couple of reasons I'm pretty sure that's part of that story. (Compare the popular modern American portrayal of both college and the military, and the nineteenth-century exhortation to "go West, young man!".)

I've just moved from a lily white suburb to a working class urban neighbourhood where white people are in the minority, and have had a fascinating problem: I keep getting asked by people still living in the suburb how I can stand living with people as racist as my new neighbours. I'm white, so it's even weirder they think it would be a massive problem, but I can't seem to get it through to them that this community is not horribly racist. I haven't tried to get them to realize that making a huge deal out of the black girl at the high school might be racist, but the thought has crossed my mind....

Is this just an enourmous amount of shadow projection or is there something else going on here?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You're dealing with the most important cognitive dissonance of our time. People in the privileged classes of American society -- that is, the upper middle class on up -- love to think of themselves as the Good People. At the same time, they benefit hugely from their own class privilege, at the expense of everyone else in society, which by their own definition makes them Bad People. So they deal with that cognitive dissonance by projecting their own privilege and prejudice onto working class people, and scream that the working class people are all horrible racists. If you want them never to speak to you again, talk about their class privilege and how their comfortable lives depend in the impoverishment of the working class people they hate. They'll drop you like a hot rock because they know that's what the score really is.

comments.title: astrology
comments.content:
This is actually a question from my 18 year old grandson. Since the constellations that form the signs of the zodiac are not visible in the southern hemisphere, why would standard astrology work for the people living there? I think I know an answer to this from some of your general comments on how astrology may work--but not well enough to explain.

He also asked if the civilizations of the southern hemisphere had their own astrology. I told him that we don't know because if they had any the Spanish would have stamped it out.

Rita

replies.title: Re: astrology
replies.content: Your 18 year old grandson needs to study his astronomy a little more closely. The constellations of the zodiac are all close to the ecliptic, and so they're just as visible from the southern hemisphere as they are from the northern hemisphere. It's the north polar constellations that aren't visible from the southern hemisphere, and none of those are part of the zodiac.

In order to have this conjunction, you have to have been born roughly between 1988 and 1998. Thus, we're in our very earlier 30s or 20s, and so old enough to be interested in sprituality. Since the conjunction was mostly in Capricorn, we're drawn to serious things, since anyone with the conjunction would have a Capricorn ascendant; weird things (Uranus); and spiritual affairs (Neptune).

Of course, this blog would be the perfect fit! Plus, a lot of people who get into astrology tend to get stuck on certain weird things, and Uranus and Neptune are both a tad under studied; plus the sheer weirdness of them mixing in Capricorn would probably confuse a lot of people. I know I spent quite a while trying to figure out just what that feature of my chart meant!
